title: Isolation
summary: Defines special isolation rules for Taiwan, overriding and extending the Series isolation rules for non-Allied, island, and Allied units.
text:
In addition to Series Isolation rules:

**11.1 Non-Allied Isolation:** If the Taiwan Straits Inshore Box is Allied Controlled, all non-Allied units on the Operational Map are Isolated regardless of their ability to trace to a Supply Source.

**11.2 Island Land Area Isolation:** Units in an Island Land Area are isolated if they cannot trace a LOC through friendly-Controlled or Contested Inshore and At Sea Boxes to a friendly Holding Box or if the opposing side has Air Supremacy.

**11.3 Allied Isolation:** If an Allied (non-ROC) unit on the Operational Map can trace to a friendly-Controlled Eastern or Northern Port, Beachhead, Airbase/Airfield Supply marker [GSR 19.1.2], US Supply Depot, or USMC HQ Supply Source [GSR 10.4.8] (regardless of the supply status of any of the above) it is not Isolated.

title: Detection
summary: Extends Series detection rules with DF-21/26 marker electronic detection and air reconnaissance detection for Taiwan.
text:
[21.0 Advanced]

**12.1 Electronic Detection:** In addition to HQs and Artillery, DF-21/26 markers can be detected using Electronic Detection [GSR 15.1.3].

**12.2 Air Reconnaissance Detection [21.2]:** Both players can only use Aerial Recon against enemy units on the Operational Map or in Island Land Areas.

Play Note: Just a reminder that these rules have changed radically, and I recommend thoroughly re-reading the Series Rules for this.

title: Airpower
summary: Game-specific airpower rules covering weather capabilities, air superiority and strikes, air unit ranges, attack coordination, air basing limitations, holding box air defenses, non-Allied ADF values, and air transport.
text:
[6.0 Standard/22.0 Advanced]

**13.1 Weather Capabilities [Standard]:** When determining Air Points in the Standard Game both sides have All-Weather Capability (i.e., for the purposes of Overcast or Storm Weather).

**13.2 Standard Game Air Superiority and Strikes [Standard]:** After rolling for and calculating Air Points, the non-Allied player may allocate any number of those Air Points to be "Strike Points". In any Standard Game scenario where the number of Strike Points is given, do not calculate them per the rules below. However, the non-Allied player may convert Air Points to Strike Points on a 2:1 basis (i.e., 2 Air Points = 1 Strike Point). Adjust the Air Superiority Level as appropriate [GSR 13.2.1].

**13.2.1 Air Superiority:** Calculate Air Superiority for the turn using the modified number of Air Points, i.e., subtract those allocated to "Strike Points" from the total.

**13.2.2 Strike Points:** To the number allocated above add 3 points per turn. Design Note: This represents Cruise and Ballistic Missiles at a very abstract level. Strike Points can be used to conduct Strikes against Beach Defenses: (1) Assign 1-6 Strike Points to the Strike. (2) The Allied player conducts ADF: -# results subtract that number of Strike Points from the Strike. (3) The non-Allied player rolls a die and consults the table: >  # of Strike Points = No Effect; ≤ # of Strike Points > ½ # of Strike Points = Marker moved 1 box right; ≤ ½ # of Strike Points = Marker moved 2 boxes right.

**13.2.3 ADF vs Strike Points:** ADF is conducted against the Strike Points as if they were Combat Support, i.e., ignore Abort results but subtract the result from the number of Strike Points assigned.

**13.3 Air Unit Ranges [Advanced]:** The range of each air unit (the letter in the upper left corner of the counter: S = Short, M = Medium, L = Long, U = Unlimited) determines how far an air unit can fly missions from its Basing Box. Air units can fly any mission they are eligible to fly under Series Rule 22.2/3. Air unit range limitations are listed on the Strategic Display. Unless otherwise specified, tracing range involves tracing movement across At Sea Boxes and/or Land Areas (if present). Do not count any Land Area associated with the Basing Box. To Strike a Holding Box, the Striking air units must be able to reach the Land Area the Holding Box represents. Exception: Malaysian air units can participate in Air Superiority. They can only Intercept or Escort in the Spratly Islands Sea Zone.

**13.3.1 Air Superiority:** Air units can fly Air Superiority missions as long as their range allows them to reach the South China Sea, Taiwan Straits, or East China Sea At Sea Boxes.

**13.3.2 Interception/Escort:** Air units can Intercept and/or Escort only those missions for which they have the appropriate range, e.g., a Medium range unit from Japan would only be able to Escort and/or Intercept into the Sea of Japan, East China Sea, Ryukyu Islands, or Northern Approaches.

**13.4 Attack Coordination [Advanced]:** US units can be assigned to the same Combat Support or Strike mission with either the CW or ROC but not both. No other Allied coordination is allowed. Units that begin in different "Ready" boxes can be part of the same Strike or Combat Support missions, assuming all units are allowed and have the Range to do so. No non-Allied coordination is allowed. Design Note: The US trains the ROCAF and works with CW nations closely. The JASDF does not, generally, train nor work closely with their US counterparts.

**13.5 Air Basing Limitations [Advanced]:** Unless otherwise specified, units can only base in their own country. Air units are restricted as to where they can be based as follows: US Air Force units can base in Japan or the ROC. Allied Carrier Air Wings [30.1] can be based in a Carrier box of their nation. PRC Carrier Air Wings can base only in their Carrier boxes. US Marine air units can base in Japan, the ROC, or in US Carrier boxes (only the optional F-35C). The US Marine F-35B can be based on a USN AMPH*. Alternatively, the USMC F-35B can be based on the UK CV or follow the rules for other US Marine air units. Commonwealth units can base in Japan or the ROC. The Japanese F-35B can base on the Japanese AMPH or in Japan. If the Philippines enter the war on the Allied side, USAF, USMC, and CW Air Units can base there. If it enters the war on the non-Allied side, PRC air units can base there. *Use the AMPH section of the US Carrier/AMPH Basing Box.

**13.5.1 SOP Designated Bases [Advanced]:** For the purposes of Steps 1a and 1b of the Air/Naval Phase, designated bases with Strike or Destroyed markers are those in the ROC and any Holding Box Airbases. ROC bases affect only the ROC Basing Box and Holding Box Airbases affect only the Basing Box of their location.

**13.5.2 Lack of Airbases/Airfield [Advanced]:** If, through a series of unfortunate events, all the Airbases or Airfields on the Operational Map or in a Holding Box are Destroyed, marked with a Strike 2 marker, or Under Repair during the Check for Airbase Destruction Effects step of the Reorganization Phase the following occur: Operational Map: Each Air Unit currently based in the associated Basing Box must either be moved to the Flown Box or immediately Rebased (if possible). ROC Air Units, in violation of GSR 13.5, can base and fly missions from Japan only if the latter has intervened at Level 2+. Such units can Rebase to the ROC as soon as there are Airbases/Airfields available. Holding Box: Half of all Air Units (owner's choice) must be moved to the Flown Box or immediately Rebased (if possible).

**13.5.3 Collateral Damage vs. non-ROC Air Units and Airmobile Points [Advanced]:** Air units can only be chosen if they are based in the Basing Box associated with the damaged/Destroyed Airbase/Airfield. Airmobile Points can only be destroyed if the Collateral Damage is against an Airbase/Airfield as follows: US: US combat units must be present on either the Operational Map or that Holding Box. Other non-ROC Allied: in that Ally's Holding Box.

**13.6 Holding Box Air Defenses [Advanced]:** The various Holding Boxes have permanent, non-reducible Air Defenses as marked in the Holding Box. During the SOF Phase and Strike Phases, a player can conduct Raids or Strikes against any Holding Box ADF values to suppress them. For Strikes, designate all the Holding Box targets normally, i.e., ADF Values or Installations. Conduct the Holding Box ADF Values Strikes first and apply any results immediately as temporary reductions to the values during this turn. The values return to the printed value during the Reorganization Phase. Players will need to devise a method of tracking the temporary reduction, e.g., Strike markers.

**13.7 Non-Allied Air Defense Values [Advanced]:** The non-Allied ADF values represent the mobile SAM and AAA batteries inherent to the units. To model that, the non-Allied ADF Track cannot be attacked with SOF Raids or Strikes, it cannot be repaired [GSR 10.3.2], nor can the non-Allied player use ADF Track values unless a non-Allied HQ is in a land hex on the Operational Map or in an Island Land Area.

**13.8 Allied Air Transport and ADF [Advanced]:** For missions on the Operational Map, unless the mission hex also meets other conditions, e.g., within two hexes of an HQ or under a Naval Umbrella [17.1.5], Allied Air Transport Missions entering Taiwan are subject to the PRC's Holding Box Air Defense Detection value. Only ED results and only Interception can take place, i.e., no SAMs or AAA.

title: Hardened Targets
summary: Identifies targets in Taiwan that are underground, specially protected, or particularly hard to hit, in addition to those in Series Rule 23.4.3.
text:
[23.0 Advanced]

These represent targets, in addition to those identified in the series rules [23.4.3], that are underground, specially protected and reinforced, or just plain hard to hit.

The ROC has two Hardened Airbases: 2219 and 3616. In addition, Beach Defense Tracks, the Brahmos marker, Typhon markers, optional S-300s, and DF-21/26 markers are Hardened Targets.

title: Theater Warfare Assets
summary: Rules for Cruise Missiles, DF-21/26 A2/AD markers, ROC Naval SSM Points, Philippines BrahMos battery, US Typhons, Ballistic Missiles, and Tactical Nuclear Weapons.
text:
[26.0 Cruise Missiles - Advanced]

Design Note: These attacks represent roughly twenty missiles, each individually targeted and programmed to fly different courses to the target. They are either flying at very low altitude with terrain following capabilities or using ballistic trajectories providing a high degree of protection from detection or attack. Given the relative lack of AWACS and the paucity of look-down/shoot-down missile-armed aircraft, it's hard to imagine a situation where at least half of the missiles would not reach the target. Thus, in game terms, Cruise/Ballistic Missiles are virtually invulnerable except to CIWS as the Strike die roll itself accounts for some loss. The adverse DRM applied to non-US strikes represents both the onboard targeting systems and, depending on the target, superior detection and antimissile defense capabilities. Cruise Missiles and Theater Weapons/Warfare Assets are generally interchangeable terms and represent a wide variety of different weapons from ballistic missiles to cruise missiles to other types of surface-to-surface missiles as well as drones.

**15.1 US and Chinese Cruise Missiles:** The US and PRC are assigned Cruise Missile points at the beginning of a scenario and/or receive them as reinforcements. Use the appropriate marker to record this on the General Records Track. Unless otherwise specified, each time a Cruise Missile Strike is initiated, including pre-game attacks, reduce the appropriate Cruise Missile points by one. Once all points have been used, no more Cruise Missile Strikes can be initiated unless additional points arrive as reinforcements.

**15.1.1 Allocation:** The attacking player announces all targets, reduces the points on the General Records track, then resolves the Strikes using the Cruise Missile column on the Strike Chart and the appropriate DRMs. Players must allocate all Cruise Missile Strikes before resolving them.

As an exception to the Series Rules, the non-Allied player can only launch Cruise Missiles (using Cruise Missile Points) against Naval Units via H-6 or H-20 [GSR 17.4.3]. See GSR 15.1.3.2 for Naval Strikes using DF-21/26s.

**15.1.1.1 Naval Units:** Each US and PRC CV/CVN or SAG unit not in Port has the capacity to launch one Cruise Missile Strike per Strike Phase. Each US AMPH unit not in Port has the capacity to launch one Cruise Missile Strike per Strike Phase.

**15.1.1.2 Air Units:** Each H-6, H-20 [GSR 17.4.3], B-52, B-1, and B-2 unit in the game can launch one Cruise Missile attack per game turn. Move the unit immediately to the Flown (or Recovery [17.3]) Box, no Interception or ADF is allowed (including pre-game attacks).

**15.1.1.3 Land-based:** The non-Allied player can use up to 5 Cruise Missiles in a Strike Phase without requiring a specific unit. Using Cruise Missiles in this way is subject to range restrictions [GSR 15.1.1.4].

**15.1.1.4 Range Restrictions:** When using an appropriate naval unit [GSR 15.1.1.1] to launch a Cruise Missile Strike, the Strike may be made to the naval unit's own or any adjacent At Sea Box (including associated Inshore Boxes and All-Sea hexes) or Land Area/Holding Box (including an Operational Map). The non-Allied player can launch Land Based Cruise Missile Strikes only against appropriate targets in Land Areas as noted on the Strategic Display. The non-Allied player can ignore range and target restrictions when using an H-6 or H-20 air unit to perform the Cruise Missile attack.

**15.1.2 Site-busting:** The PRC possesses land-based Theater Weapons allowing the Allied player to attack [26.1.3] the PRC Cruise Missile marker per 26.1.3. It is a Hardened Target. The US Cruise Missile marker may not be attacked.

**15.1.3 DF-21/26 and non-Allied A2/AD (Anti-Access/Area Denial) [Advanced]:** These four DF-21/26 markers are placed in the PRC Holding Box in all Advanced Game Scenarios. They can be moved, using Air or Sea Transport, to the Spratly Islands, Paracels, or Senkakus Island Land Areas (if non-Allied Controlled), or Malaysia or the Philippines if either of the latter enters the war on the non-Allied side. Each DF-21/26 marker can use its capabilities [GSR 15.1.3.2, GSR 15.1.3.3, GSR 15.1.3.4] twice per turn, but only once per phase.

**15.1.3.1 Detection, Damage, and Destruction:** The DF-21/26 markers are Hardened Targets. They must be Detected by Electronic Detection, SOF Recon (as HQ/SAM), or be adjacent to an enemy ground unit (only possible in an Island Land Area) before they can be attacked. They follow the rules for Installations regarding SOF Raids and Strikes [GSR 6.5.4.1]. They can be damaged and Destroyed by SOF Raids or Strikes. If alone in an Island Land Area with enemy units, they are also Destroyed. Strike results are DRMs applied against any Strikes it conducts, e.g., a Strike 1 marker on the DF-21/26 results in a +1 DRM when it makes a Strike. Strikes affect them the same as HQs/Artillery [18.2.6]. If there are restrictions on GT1 SOF Raids and/or Strikes, then these markers can only be attacked if the ADF Track is also specified as being eligible to be attacked. Strike marker reductions are conducted normally during the Reorganization Phase. Destroyed markers can be returned to the game. The non-Allied player can return one marker per turn during the Reorganization Phase by placing the marker on the Game Information Track as a reinforcement two turns ahead. If more than one marker is Destroyed, those can be returned in future turns via the process above. Each marker can only be replaced once. When it returns, place it on its striped replacement side. An already replaced marker, when eliminated, is removed from the game.

**15.1.3.2 Naval Strikes:** During the Cruise/Ballistic Missile segment (b) of each Strike Phase, each DF-21/26 marker can conduct one Strike as a Naval SSM Strike against a detected Naval Unit up to two At Sea Boxes away, as noted on the counter, or any Inshore Box or All-Sea hex associated with those At Sea Boxes. This is conducted as a "Naval 3" Strike, and it does not expend Missile Points of any kind.

**15.1.3.3 Affecting Sea Movement:** When the Allied player attempts to move a Naval Unit or use Sea Transport, the non-Allied player can use a DF-21/26 to influence the move. If he does, this has two effects: (1) the move attempt is automatically resolved as Contested Sea Movement, and (2) the Allied player incurs an additional "+1" DRM to the Contested Sea Movement die roll, as noted on the chart. Only movement into or out of areas to which the chosen DF-21/26 has range [GSR 15.1.3.2] can be affected.

**15.1.3.4 Affecting Sea Control:** The non-Allied player can use a DF-21/26 to affect Sea Control DRMs (both At Sea and associated Inshore Boxes). For each die roll on which a DF-21/26 is used, apply a DRM as follows (these DRMs are not cumulative): −2 at range 0 to 1 from the DF-21/26; −1 at range 2 from the DF-21/26.

**15.2 ROC Naval SSM Points:** Like the non-Allied player, the Allied player can elect to spend Naval SSM Points to affect Sea Movement and Control but can only do so in the Taiwan Straits At Sea and Inshore Boxes. In addition, the Allied player can conduct Naval SSM Strikes using Naval SSM Points.

**15.2.1 Affecting Sea Movement:** When the non-Allied player attempts to move a Naval Unit or use Sea Transport, the Allied player can reduce the Naval SSM Point marker by one. This has two effects: (1) the move attempt is automatically resolved as Contested Sea Movement, and (2) the non-Allied player incurs an additional "+1" DRM to the Contested Sea Movement die roll, as noted on the chart. The Allied player can only affect movement into or out of the Taiwan Straits At Sea, Taiwan Straits Inshore Box, or associated All-Sea hexes.

**15.2.2 Affecting Sea Control:** The Allied player can expend a Naval SSM Point to affect the Sea Control roll for the Taiwan Straits At Sea or Inshore Box. For each die roll on which a Naval SSM Point is expended, apply a +1 DRM (as noted on the chart).

**15.2.3 Naval Strikes:** During the Cruise/Ballistic Missile segment (b) of each Strike Phase, the Allied player can expend Naval SSM Points to conduct Naval SSM Strikes against detected Naval Units in the Taiwan Straits At Sea, Taiwan Straits Inshore Box, or associated All-Sea hexes. Each point spent allows one Strike. This is a "Naval 2" Strike.

**15.2.3 Site-busting:** The ROC Naval SSM Points marker can be attacked [26.1.3]. It is a Hardened Target.

**15.3 Philippines BrahMos Cruise Missile Battery:** This counter represents the BrahMos cruise missiles purchased by the Philippines. If the Philippines enter the war, that side places the battery counter in the Philippines and places the matching Cruise Missile Points marker on the 5 space. It can be used to cause a Contested Sea Movement penalty for the opposing player, and it can conduct Naval Strikes, just as the PRC DF-21/26 can [GSR 15.1.3.2], whether Allied or non-Allied. It can use its capabilities in any adjacent At Sea Box or associated Inshore Box as noted on the counter. The battery must be Detected by Electronic Detection or SOF Recon before it can be attacked by Strikes or SOF Raids. The battery is a Hardened Target. It is removed from the game if it is Destroyed or all Cruise Missiles are expended. Do not reduce Strike markers on the BrahMos battery during the Reorganization Phase.

**15.3.1 Site-busting:** The PH Cruise Missile Points marker can be a target per 26.1.3. It is a Hardened Target.

**15.4 US Typhons:** These counters each represent a battery of the US Strategic Mid-Range Fires System. To be used, a Typhon counter must either be set up or be shipped into a friendly Holding Box or Island Land area using one point of Sea Transport. Any Strike result from Contested Sea Movement destroys the battery while in transit. If the US intervenes at Level 3+, a battery can be shipped into a Port on the Taiwan Operational Map. If the battery arrives safely, it can be placed in any hex which can trace an LOC of any length, using roads, to the Port. The battery can be used to cause or apply a DRM to a Contested Sea Movement roll, affect Sea Control, and conduct Naval SSM Strikes, similar to the PRC DF-21/26 except it can only use this ability once per turn [GSR 15.1.3]. The battery can use its capabilities in any adjacent At Sea Box or its associated Inshore Box/All-Sea hex. The battery must be Detected by Electronic Detection, SOF Recon, or an adjacent ground unit before it can be attacked by Strikes or SOF Raids. A battery is a Hardened Target. It is removed from the game if it is Destroyed. Do not reduce Strike markers on the Typhon batteries during the Reorganization Phase.

**15.5 Ballistic Missiles [Advanced]:** Each Advanced Game scenario assigns Ballistic Missile points to both sides. Use the appropriate marker to record this on the General Records Track. Each time a Ballistic Missile attack is initiated, including pre-game attacks, reduce the appropriate Ballistic Missile points by one. Each side can use up to 5 ballistic missile points per Strike Phase if available.

**15.5.1 Site-Busting:** Each player can target the other side's Missile marker with appropriate Strikes. It is a Hardened Target.

**15.5.2 Range Restrictions:** Ballistic missiles can only be used against targets in hexes on the Operational Map or the PRC Holding Box.

**15.6 Tactical Nuclear Weapons [Advanced]:**

**15.6.1 Nuclear Weapon Release:** In Next War: Taiwan neither player begins with nuclear weapon release. Once release is granted, a player can use nuclear weapons in any Strike Phase.

**15.6.1.1 PRC Release:** The PRC is granted nuclear weapon release in the Strike Phase immediately following one of these circumstances: The Allied player conducts a US Cruise Missile and/or Air Strike with US air units in the PRC Holding Box; The Allied player eliminates a PRC CV/CVN; The Allied player eliminates a PRC Beachhead; The Allied player has more VP than the non-Allied player AND has Initiative and/or is First Player for the turn; The Allied player successfully resolves a nuclear attack marker (due to #2 or #3 below).

**15.6.1.2 US Release:** If the US has intervened and not Withdrawn [GSR 157], the Allied player is automatically granted nuclear weapon release in the following circumstances: In the same Strike Phase that the non-Allied player places [GSR 15.6.3.2] his first Nuclear Attack marker; In the Strike Phase immediately following the non-Allied player's first use of Chemical Weapons [GSR 17.11]; In the Strike Phase immediately following the non-Allied player's first roll on the Automatic Victory table. Once the Allied player has nuclear weapon release, Allied nuclear weapons are available for use in any Strike Phase.

**15.6.2 Nuclear Weapon Points:** Both players start with a specified number of Nuclear Weapon Points, as given by the scenario allocations. These points are never replenished.

**15.6.3 Nuclear Weapon Attacks:**

**15.6.3.1 Placing a Nuclear Attack Marker:** In either Strike Phase, prior to all other Strikes, each player (alternating, Initiative/First Player first) can place Nuclear Attack Markers. If the non-Initiative/First player initiates the use of nuclear weapons, the other player may respond in that Strike Phase. A side can place as many Nuclear Attack markers per turn as it wishes. Decrement the player's Nuclear Weapons Points marker by one for each Nuclear Attack marker placed. The non-Allied player can place a Nuclear Attack marker in any hex on the Operational Map (including an All-Sea hex) or Island Land Area containing Detected [21.0] Allied units, an Allied Controlled Installation/Airfield in Taiwan or Holding Box, or any Detected Naval Unit in an At Sea/Inshore Box (with no range limitations). A marker placed in the Japan or Guam Holding box affects the Global Thermonuclear War die roll [GSR 15.6.8]; increases the US Intervention Level to 4 (even if previously Withdrawn [GSR 15.7]); i.e., the US re-enters the war, and no Allied nation will Withdraw. The Allied player can place a Nuclear Attack marker in any hex on the Operational Map (including an All-Sea hex) or Island Land Area containing Detected [21.0] non-Allied units, a non-Allied Controlled Installation/Airfield in Taiwan, an Installation in the PRC Holding Box, or any Detected Naval Unit in an At Sea/Inshore Box (with no range limitations). The Allied player cannot place such a marker in Urban/City hexes. See GSR 15.6.8 for the effect of a marker being placed on an Installation in the PRC Holding Box. Once each player finishes placing his markers, he rolls for Global Thermonuclear War. If the game doesn't end after either roll, then resolve them.

**15.6.3.2 Resolving a Nuclear Attack:** Once all markers are allocated to target hexes, and after rolling for Global Thermonuclear War [GSR 15.6.8], resolve each Nuclear Attack marker by rolling one die: On a 0–8 the attack result is "full yield" with the following results: vs. Hex or Island Land Area: Each enemy non-embarked ground unit, Supply Depot, or MSU in the target hex or Island Land Area loses one step (one step units are eliminated). Any surviving non-embarked ground units and naval units in a Port receive a Strike 2 marker. Apply appropriate results to embarked ground units. All Fortifications, Installations, and Airfields in the hex are marked destroyed. All bridge hexsides are destroyed. Reduce Beach Defense markers by one if the hex is a Beach [GSR 2.4]. vs Naval Unit: An attacked naval unit and any embarked ground units or CAW are destroyed. VP and Collateral Damage are assessed normally. On a roll of 9, roll again: if the roll is even (0, 2, 4, 6, 8), treat it as "full yield" as above; if the roll is odd (1, 3, 5, 7, 9) the attack result is "malfunction". Remove the Nuclear Attack marker. There is no further effect.

**15.6.4 Nuclear Weapon Attack Markers:** Once successfully resolved (i.e., "full yield"), a Nuclear Attack marker is never removed. In addition, it has the following effects:

**15.6.4.1 Efficiency Rating Penalty:** The ER of a unit is affected by proximity to the hex as follows: Ground Zero: The Ground Zero hex is the hex with the Nuclear Attack marker in it. Any non-Allied or ROC unit in a hex or Island Land Area in a Ground Zero hex has their efficiency reduced by 4. All other units have their efficiency reduced by 3. ER can never be reduced to less than 1. This effect does not apply to non-Island Land Areas. Fallout: Any unit in a hex adjacent to Ground Zero has their efficiency reduced by 2. This effect does not apply to Island Land Areas.

**15.6.4.2 Movement Penalty:** Leg or light infantry class units can only enter a Ground Zero hex using Ground Movement by spending their entire movement allowance. Motorized or mechanized class units spend an additional +4 MPs to enter such a hex. This does not apply to Island Land Areas.

**15.6.4.3 Supply Path Effects:** No Line of Communication [19.2] can be traced into/through a Ground Zero hex for determining supply. Units can still trace out of the hex to a potential Supply Source. This does not apply to Island Land Areas.

**15.6.4.4 At Sea/Inshore:** There is no lingering effect in At Sea/Inshore Boxes. The marker remains on the Strategic Display, however, for Global Thermonuclear War [GSR 15.6.8] determination purposes.

**15.6.5 Nuclear Weapon Facilities:** Destroying Nuclear Weapon Facilities has no effect on the number of Nuclear Weapon Points available for a side. Destroyed Facilities do count for Victory Points.

**15.6.6 Collateral Damage DRM:** If a Nuclear Attack is successfully resolved [GSR 15.6.3.2] in a hex with an enemy Airbase or Airfield, the Collateral Damage [27.7.4] roll incurs a −2 DRM.

**15.6.7 Nuclear Weapon Attack VP:** For both players, successful resolution [GSR 15.6.3.2] of a Nuclear Attack awards the other player ten VP. If the Allied player is the first to use nuclear weapons, the non-Allied player earns an additional ten VP. This is added directly to their VP total and not to the "VPs This Turn". If a Nuclear Attack marker is placed in an enemy Urban or City hex, an additional ten VP (for a total of 20VP) are awarded to the other player. In addition, all VP that result from a Nuclear Attack (eliminated enemy units, Installations, Collateral Damage, etc.) are added directly to VP totals and not to "VPs This Turn".

**15.6.8 Global Thermonuclear War (aka Lighter Fluid Rule, aka, Would You Like to Play a Game? - aka Game Over, Man, Game Over):** After placing all his Nuclear Attack markers, but before rolling to resolve any [GSR 15.6.3.2], that player rolls a die. Apply the following DRMs: +2 to Allied roll if the non-Allied player has used Chemical Weapons [GSR 17.11]; −2 to Allied roll if a Nuclear Attack marker has been placed in Guam or the Japan Holding Box; −2 to non-Allied roll if a Nuclear Attack marker has been placed in the PRC Holding Box that turn. If the modified die roll is less than or equal to the total number of Nuclear Attack markers (resolved and unresolved) currently on the map, the game immediately ends in Global Thermonuclear War. Technically, the player making the roll loses the game.

**15.7 Withdrawal Check:** Use of PRC nuclear weapons may cause Allied nationalities to Withdraw if they have Intervened. At the end of the first Strike Phase in which a Nuclear Attack is successfully resolved [GSR 15.6.3.2] by the non-Allied player, roll a die for each intervening nation. If any of a nation's unit were affected by the attack, add the total number of markers placed as a DRM to that die roll and subtract that nation's current Intervention Level. Non-US forces withdraw from the conflict on a modified roll of 6 or more. US forces withdraw from the conflict on a modified roll of 9 or more. Any nation that does not withdraw increases its Intervention Level. If any of its units were affected by the attack, set its Intervention Level to 4; otherwise, increase it by two. When the forces of a nation withdraw, that nation's ground units, air units, Cruise Missile points, Nuclear Weapon points, Supply Depots, SOF mission markers, and Airmobile Points are moved to Japan (US only) or removed from the game as follows in the rules below, and future reinforcements for that nation do not arrive. For all purposes, for that nation, the intervention level is set to Level 0 - No Intervention. Exception: Any VPs awarded at the start of the game to the opposing player under the intervention level selection rules [GSR 18.3.1.2] are retained.

**15.7.1 Ground Units:** They cannot conduct any attacks but defend normally.

**15.7.2 Air Units:** They cannot conduct any Air [23.4.1] or Cruise Missile [26.0] Strikes. They can conduct Combat Support missions [23.5], but only in support of defending friendly ground units of the same nationality. If in the Air Superiority box, they can conduct Interceptions [24.4].

**15.7.3 Attack Helicopters:** They cannot conduct any Helicopter Strikes [23.4.2]. They can conduct Combat Support missions [23.5], but only in support of defending friendly ground units of the same nationality.

**15.7.4 Naval Units:** They cannot conduct Cruise Missile Strikes, Naval Surface Combat, or Naval Bombardment. They can conduct Combat Support, but only in support of defending friendly ground units of the same nationality.

**15.7.5 Game Information Display:** A nation's markers (including SOF Mission markers), if any, remain on the display but cannot be used.

**15.7.6 Disposition of Units:** At the end of the Victory Determination Phase of the turn when the withdrawal is triggered, remove all units of that nation from the map or Holding Boxes. US units are placed in Japan.

title: UN Resolution and Automatic Victory
summary: Specifies conditions for Automatic Victory rolls and veto procedures for UN Resolutions in the Taiwan scenario.
text:
[12.2 Standard/28.0 Advanced]

**16.1 Automatic Victory:** To roll for Automatic Victory, the non-Allied player must Control a non-Destroyed Port and have combat units [2.2.2] on the Operational Map.

**16.2 Vetoes:** The non-Allied player may always veto the Resolution. If that player chooses not to do so, then the Allied player may veto the Resolution regardless of the US Intervention Level [GSR 17.7].

title: Optional Rules
summary: A comprehensive collection of optional rules covering Allied airpower variants, refugees, US readiness, PRC air/naval power, Commonwealth intervention, UN mandate, weather, scenario start options, chemical weapons, special equipment, and other game balance adjustments.
text:
**17.1 Allied Airpower:**

Design Note: The US now has 3 active and operational F-35 squadrons, so, it's not conjectural anymore. The option remains, however, as there's no guarantee those particular CAWs will be on station. The cost has been reduced, though...

**17.1.1 USN F-35Cs [Advanced; +3 to +9 VP non-Allied]:** The Allied player can replace any one F/A-18E or F/A-18F per Carrier Air Wing [30.1] with one of the optional F-35Cs (there are more CAWs than F-35Cs). The non-Allied player gets +3 VP for each optional aircraft added.

**17.1.2 USMC F-35C [Advanced; +3 VP non-Allied]:** On occasion a Marine squadron will replace a Navy squadron on a US carrier during a deployment. There is a single optional USMC F-35C in Next War: Taiwan that may also be used to replace a USN F/A-18E or F/A-18F (allowing the replacement of 4 total).

**17.1.3 USMC F-35B [Advanced; +0 VP non-Allied]:** At the Allied player's option, one USMC F-35B may be based on a USN AMPH. Use the US AMPH Basing Box with the appropriate number for the AMPH. Alternatively, the USMC F-35B may be based on the UK CV (if the CW intervenes at Level 2+).

**17.1.4 Additional Wild Weasels:**

**17.1.4.1 USMC AH-1Z [Advanced; +10 VP non-Allied]:** The USMC AH-1Zs can carry ARMs (Anti-Radiation Missiles). The Allied player may use this option to allow AH-1Zs to conduct Wild Weasel Strikes against Detection and SAM tracks. They can only be used for this if the non-Allied player has HQs in any hex in Taiwan. The AH-1Z must be in a hex on the Operational Map (on or an AMPH) or in the Taiwan Straits Inshore Box on an AMPH. This uses one Combat Support mission.

**17.1.4.2 US F-22 and F-35 [Advanced; +15 VP non-Allied]:** US F-22s and F-35s use the Wild Weasel column on the Strike Table when conducting Strikes against the Air Defense Track.

**17.1.5 Allied Combat Support Restrictions [Advanced; +10 VP Allied]:** The Allied player may choose to restrict Combat Support [23.5] missions. Air, Attack Helicopter, and Naval Units providing Combat Support for a stack of ground units that does not include at least one unit of their nationality, halve their Combat Support value (rounded down). US and CW units are treated as one nationality for this rule. In addition, ROC air units are limited to one air unit plus escorts and/or defense suppression aircraft when conducting Combat Support.

**17.1.6 F-22 Air Unit Replacement [Advanced; +10 VP Non-Allied]:** In addition to the unit types listed in GSR 8.4, the US player may also replace F-22 air units. The cost to replace F-22s is three Supply Points per step instead of two.

**17.2 Refugees (Internally Displaced Persons) [Standard/Advanced; +0 VP]:** If both players agree that this rule is in effect, the road net in the ROC is clogged with people fleeing the embattled forces in and around the landing site(s). The GTs listed are in reference to the turn non-Allied combat units [2.2.2] first enter a hex on the Operational Map, i.e., GT1 is the turn of landing. GT 1 & 2 Invader: Non-road movement point costs are x1½; Primary Roads are treated as Secondary Roads; Highways are treated as Primary Roads. GT 1 & 2 Defender: Non-road movement point costs are doubled; Primary Roads cost 1½ MPs; Highways are treated as Secondary Roads. GT 3 & 4 Invader: All costs normal. GT 3 & 4 Defender: Non-road movement point costs are x1½; Primary Roads are treated as Secondary Roads; Highways are treated as Primary Roads. GT 5 Invader: All costs normal. GT 5 Defender: All costs normal.

**17.3 US Readiness [Standard/Advanced; +15 VP Allied]:** The Allied player reduces US combat unit Efficiency Ratings by one for the duration of the scenario except when conducting Clearing Operations. This option can only be chosen if the US intervenes at Level 3+.

**17.4 PRC Air and Naval Power:**

**17.4.1 PRC Carrier Stealth Air Units [Advanced; +4 to +12 VP Allied]:** News reports are showing a carrier-based variant of the J-31 dubbed the J-35. Three counters are provided, one for each PRC CV BG (including the optional one below [GSR 17.4.5]). The Allied player receives +4 VP for each optional aircraft added. This changes the PRC CAW for the non-optional CVs to 1 x J-15 and 1 x J-35.

**17.4.2 PRC Stealth Fighters [Advanced; +10 VP Allied]:** The PRC J-31 does not have a firm projected in-service date. The non-Allied player may choose this option to include both J-31 air units at start in the PRC Ready Box.

**17.4.3 PRC Stealth Bomber [Advanced; +10 VP Allied]:** The PRC H-20 is expected to enter service sometime around 2033. The non-Allied player may choose this option to include the H-20 unit. It starts all scenarios in the Ready Box.

**17.4.4 PRC Combat Support Restrictions [Advanced; +5 VP non-Allied]:** As an additional optional rule, the non-Allied player can choose to be restricted to only commit a single air unit, along with escorts, to CS missions. Design Note: The PLAAF and PLA do not embrace CAS as understood in Western doctrine as evidenced given their weapon load out choice, training, and reliance on SSMs. If players wish to operate with Chinese doctrine, then invoke this rule.

**17.4.5 Additional PRC Carrier [Advanced; +10 VP Allied]:** Choosing this option allows the non-Allied player to use the third PRC CVN BG (#3). This carrier operates the optional J-15 included in the countermix and may, optionally, also operate one J-35 [GSR 17.4.1], i.e., its CAW is 1 x J-15 and 1 x J-35.

**17.4.6 Russian Parts Contract [Advanced; +x VP Allied]:** The PRC can choose to use this option before the game begins to allow air unit replacements, in addition to the list in GSR 8.4, for its Su-30MMk2s and Su-35. For every 3VP added to the Allied VP Total add 2 RU Air Replacement Supply Points. These points can be used as normal to replace those air units.

**17.5 PRC Marines [Standard/Advanced; +5 to +10 VP Allied]:** The PRC 5 and 6 Marine brigades are not listed as setup or reinforcements. The non-Allied player may add them as a reinforcement in any turn after GT 4. For each Marine brigade that enters the game add 5 VP to the Allied Total VPs.

**17.6 Commonwealth Intervenes [Standard: +30 VP non-Allied; Advanced: Variable]:** In the Standard Campaign Game, the Allied player may choose to have the Commonwealth intervene. Commonwealth units arrive per the Master Allied Reinforcement Chart. In the Standard Game, all Commonwealth (UK and AU) units are available, and the Allied player receives an additional 6 Air Points each turn. For the Advanced Game, see 18.3.1.2.

**17.7 UN Mandate [Advanced; +10 VP non-Allied]:** The Allied player may choose to use this option. Every time the non-Allied player Vetoes [28.1] a UN Resolution or Refuses to Obey a Ceasefire [28.2], increase the US and CW Intervention Level by one until it reaches Level 4. The non-Allied player earns 10 VP each time this option is exercised regardless of Intervention Level increase.

**17.8 Weather Control Satellites [Standard/Advanced; +10 VP Allied]:** The non-Allied player may choose this option. If the last turn was Storm, and Storm is rolled again for the current turn, it becomes Overcast instead. In other words, you cannot have two Storm turns in a row.

**17.9 Pacific Typhoon [Standard/Advanced; +0 VP]:** Both players may agree to roll for weather for each At Sea Box. Roll for weather in the following order: South China Sea, Spratly Islands, Taiwan Straits (main map weather), Philippine Sea, East China Sea, Central Approaches, Northern Approaches, Celebes, and Marianas. If Storm is rolled for any At Sea Box see 4.1 for effects. If they choose to retreat they do so as follows: If Allied: retreat to the Japan or US Holding Box; If non-Allied: retreat to a friendly Holding Box.

**17.9.1 Ride it Out [Advanced; +0 VP]:** In the Advanced Game, during a Storm Turn Naval Units, instead of retreating, can stay in a Contested or enemy Controlled At Sea Box and have a Strike 1 marker placed on them.

**17.10 Coup de Main Scenario Start [Advanced; +10-40 VP Allied]:** As an alternative start for all Advanced Game scenarios, the non-Allied player may choose to use this option to automatically take Control of Penghu, the Paracels, the Senkakus, and/or the Spratly Islands. Place one PRC Marine brigade (from 1, 2, 3, and 4 Marines, in that order) in each Island Land Area chosen for this option. Place one PRC AMPH, a non-Allied Control marker, and, optionally, any one other available PRC Naval Unit, in each associated Inshore Box of the Island Land Area chosen. Eliminate the ROC 77th Marine Brigade if Penghu is chosen. If all four locations are chosen: The non-Allied player receives the 4 Marine brigade at setup for this purpose (and, obviously, it's removed from the Reinforcement Schedule), and The non-Allied player automatically receives the PRC 5 and 6 Marine brigades [GSR 17.5] at no additional cost. All game effects such as entry into the war and increases in Intervention Levels are performed as normal. The non-Allied player receives pre-game VP per the VP schedule on the map for controlling Island Land Areas, and, if Penghu is chosen, additional VP for eliminating the ROC 77th. The Allied player receives 10 VP per Island Land Area chosen.

**17.11 Chemical Weapons [Advanced; variable VP]:** The non-Allied player can use Chemical Weapons. There are three PRC Chemical Weapons markers. They are used as per 18.2.4. Each use of a marker provides the Allied player with 5 VP. Any VP earned is added directly to the VP total and not to the "VP This Turn". To determine how many CW Points the non-Allied player has, roll one die, halve it (rounding down) and add five to the result, e.g., a roll of two equals six CW Points. The use of CW affects the Global Thermonuclear War die roll.

**17.12 Low Frequency Radar [Advanced; +10 VP non-Allied]:** The non-Allied player can choose this option. If chosen, then the DRM for detecting a mission composed solely of "Stealth" units, for both sides, becomes +3 (instead of +5).

**17.13 Guam, (a) Rock (in) of the Pacific [Advanced; +10 VP non-Allied]:** The Allied player may choose this option, and, if he does, the Airbase in Guam is Hardened.

**17.14 J-7 Attack Drones [Advanced; +5 VP Allied]:** The non-Allied player can choose this option to conduct five J-7 drone Strikes during the designated Scenario Special Rule step. Eligible targets are any valid Ballistic or Cruise Missile target in the Taiwan Straits At Sea Box, Inshore Box, associated All-Sea hex, or in any hex on the Operational Map. These are in addition to other missile strikes, they are subject to Normal ADF (unlike other Ballistic or Cruise Missiles) and may be escorted by the non-Allied and intercepted by the Allied player (these escorts/interceptors come from the PRC/ROC Ready Box and are placed in the Flown Box after resolution). It is treated as a Cruise Missile for all other purposes.

**17.15 Additional Beachhead Assets [Advanced; +10 VP Allied]:** If the non-Allied player chooses this option, the second PRC Beachhead is available for use [GSR 2.5.2].

**17.16 THAAD [Advanced; +10 VP non-Allied]:** If using the THAAD rules from the Supplements, the Allied player can transport a THAAD in Taiwan if the US intervenes at Level 3+. It must start in the US Holding Box and use Sea Transport to move to a friendly Port in Taiwan. It does not count against Port Capacity.

**17.17 Fifth Generation Operations [Advanced; +12 VP Allied]:** Choosing this option allows the non-Allied player to conduct four Naval Bombardments from any Amphibious Assault Zone All-Sea hexes as a Naval 3 with a range of 2 hexes once per game during any one Strike Phase.

**17.18 ROC Reserves [Standard/Advanced; +3 non-Allied VP]:** The Allied player may choose to use this optional rule. When drawing a ROC Reserve unit as a reinforcement, the player may, instead, receive 1 or 2 Replacement Points. If 1 REPL is chosen, the Reserve unit arrives with one step loss. If 2 REPLs are chosen, the Reserve unit is removed from the game. In addition, if a ROC Reserve unit is stacked with any other non-Armor ROC combat unit during the Reinforcement and Replacement Phase, the Allied player may transfer steps from the ROC reserve unit to the other unit. [See the Design Note in GSR 8.1.2.]

**17.19 Restrictive Terrain [Advanced; +0 VP]:** The terrain in Taiwan's mountainous areas is restrictive in terms of "off-roading". For a better portrayal of this, if both players agree, apply the following additional effects to Mechanized and Motorized units in Mountain hexes: −2 DRM to all Strikes against such units; If all attacking units are Mechanized and/or Motorized, shift one column left.

**17.20 Hellscape:**

**17.20.1 Unmanned Surface Vessels [Advanced; +5 VP non-Allied]:** If the Allied player chooses this option, then, during any Combat Segment, as part of Naval Surface Combat, a player, non-Allied first, can conduct a Naval 2 Surface Combat [17.3] attack against any Point Detected Naval Unit in an All-Sea hex. These attacks occur before any other Naval Surface Combat. The attacked Naval Unit must be with 10 hexes of a friendly-Controlled Port. The Allied player can only use this if either the US or Japan intervenes at Level 2+. Each player can only do this four times.

**17.20.2 Replicator Swarms [Advanced; +15 VP non-Allied]:** If the US intervenes at Level 2+, the Allied player can choose this option. During step 7b of each Strike Phase, both players, non-Allied first, can conduct one drone swarm Strike against any one Point Detected Naval Unit in an All-Sea hex or in the Taiwan Straits Inshore Box. This Strike is a US or PRC Naval 2 SSM Strike.

**17.21 PRC S-300s [Advanced; +15 VP Allied]:** The non-Allied side is restricted in using ADF Track values in Taiwan [GSR 13.7]. If the PRC chooses to use this rule, place the two optional PRC S-300 counters in the PRC Holding Box. If both are operational, i.e., not Destroyed, the non-Allied player can use Normal or Local Detection against eligible Allied missions on the Operational Map as if the Allied mission were in an enemy country using the ADF values on the ADF Track. The PRC player must trace a straight line down a column of hexes from the map edge to the target hex, and this line cannot enter more than one Mountain hex. If Detected, the Allied mission is subject to Interception and SAM ADF as applicable. The mission is only subject to AAA ADF if it is within two hexes of a non-Allied combat unit, HQ, Artillery, or non-Allied Controlled Installation. S-300s are Hardened Targets. Strike marker reductions are conducted normally during the Reorganization Phase. They can be Repaired in the same fashion as DF-21/26s [GSR 15.1.3.1], if Destroyed. If either or both S-300s are Destroyed, the non-Allied player reverts to the printed ADF values in the PRC Holding Box and is subject to the restrictions of GSR 13.7 until both are fully operational again.

**17.22 Narco-boats [Advanced; +5 non-Allied VP]:** If the Allied player chooses this option, then USMC units in Island Land Areas are always in supply [GSR 5.1.4.9] and never isolated [GSR 11.2].

**17.23 PRC "Mulberry" Barges [Standard/Advanced; +10 VP Allied]:** If the PRC chooses to use this option, the Mulberry Barge counter can be placed in any Movement Segment after a Beachhead has been placed. It allows, in addition to normal Beachhead usage, 2 Stacking Points of Motorized and Mechanized units to use Sea Transport to land at the Beachhead per turn. It can be the target of SOF Targeting and Raids, Air and Helicopter Strikes, and Cruise and Ballistic Missile Strikes. A Strike 1 or 2 marker has no effect. Once Destroyed, it cannot be repaired nor rebuilt.

title: Scenarios
summary: Scenario setup rules, standard and advanced scenarios for Next War: Taiwan, including force setup, reinforcement schedules, special scenario rules, intervention levels, and victory conditions.
text:
Scenarios are divided into two types: Standard and Advanced. Standard scenarios generally examine smaller actions within only a portion of the map, use fewer playing pieces, and are shorter. There is also a full map, full game scenario. These are all played with only the Standard Game Rules. Advanced Scenarios involve the entire map, the Strategic Display, use all the pieces (potentially), and will take longer to play.

**18.1 Scenario Setups:** Unless otherwise specified within a scenario, the Allies set up first, followed by the non-Allies. Use the following guidelines when setting up forces for both Standard and Advanced scenarios:

**18.1.1 PRC Setup:** Unless otherwise specified, all scenario forces should set up in the PRC Holding Box or the PRC Basing Box as appropriate.

**18.1.2 ROC Setup:** ROC units must set up within the areas listed below based on their Theater Command (as shown on the map): 3rd Theater Command (3 TC): On or North of the 40xx hex row. 4th Theater Command (4 TC): On or South of the 25xx hex row. 5th Theater Command (5 TC): Between the 26xx and 39xx hex rows (inclusive).

**18.1.2.1 Movement Penalty:** ROC units must set up within the areas listed below based on their Theater Command. This movement penalty applies during setup.

**18.1.2.2 Other Restrictions:** Within the restrictions of the Theater Command setup areas above, all units set up one unit per hex (Exception: HQs may stack). Non-Reserve (A or C) ground units in any Urban or City hex; Reserve ground units in any Urban, City, or Installation hex.

**18.1.2.3 Independent Units:** Hualien Mech Inf Reg in 3616. Taitung Mech Inf Reg in 2118.

**18.1.2.4 Attack Helicopters:** ROC Attack Helicopters can set up in any friendly Airbase or Airfield in Taiwan.

**18.1.2.5 Surface Action Groups:** [Advanced] Unless directed otherwise, the ROC SAGs may set up in any friendly ROC Port, any All-Sea hex, or the Taiwan Straits Inshore Box. If they start in the Inshore Box, they are automatically Area Detected and the pre-game Detection Roll cannot cause the PRC to lose Detection, i.e., it can only get better or remain the same.

**18.1.2.6 ROC Marines:** The ROC Marine brigades set up as indicated on their counters. The 77th Brigade (Penghu) will only be used when the Strategic Display is in play.

**18.1.2.7 Beach Defense Tracks [GSR 5.5]:** Use the points provided by the scenario to increase the Beach Defense in each AAZ in the play area.

**18.1.2.8 ROC Movement Restrictions:** In all scenarios, unless otherwise directed by SSR, no Allied ground unit can move from its starting hex (after any pre-game movement) until a non-Allied ground unit has: attempted (successful or not) Airmobile or Airborne Movement into a hex in Taiwan or in Penghu; attempted to Amphibiously Assault into a coastal hex on Taiwan or on Penghu (including via Coup de Main [GSR 17.10]).

**18.1.3 Sea Control:** Place control markers as follows: Non-Allied Control: Gulf of Tonkin, Taiwan Straits, South China Sea and East China Sea At Sea Boxes; Yellow Sea Inshore Box. Allied Control: None. Contested: Northern & Central Approaches, Marianas, Sea of Japan, Philippines Sea, Celebes Sea, Ryukyu Islands, and Spratly Islands At Sea Boxes; East Sea, Spratly Islands, Paracels, and Taiwan Straits Inshore Boxes. (The absence of a Control Marker indicates a Contested State.) Island Land Area Control: Unless otherwise directed by scenario: Penghu: Controlled by the Allied player in all scenarios. Senkakus: If Japan Intervenes at Level 1+, Controlled by Japan; otherwise, uncontrolled [GSR 18.3.3.2]. Paracels: Uncontrolled. Spratly Islands: Uncontrolled. Optional and Scenario Special Rules can alter this starting status.

**18.2 Standard Scenarios:**

**18.2.1 Target Kaohsiung:** This scenario represents a PRC landing in the south and an attempt to capture Kaohsiung. It uses only a portion of the map and units. It has special Victory Conditions. Game Length: 4 Turns (2 weeks). Play Area: All hexes on/south of hex row 22xx. Exception: Helicopter units can base in any eligible hex on the map. Initiative: The non-Allied player has the Initiative for GTs 1, 2, and 3. GT4 is Contested. Air Points given per table. Strike Points: 4 per turn. Special Victory Conditions apply using custom VP chart.

**18.2.2 Target Taichung:** This scenario represents an invasion in the middle of Taiwan. It uses only a portion of the map and units. It has special Victory Conditions. Game Length: 4 Turns (2 weeks). Play Area: All hexes on/north of hex row 23xx and on/south of hex row 40xx.

**18.2.3 Target Taipei:** This scenario represents the PRC invasion more to the north of Taiwan with a target of Taipei. It uses only a portion of the map and unit. It has special Victory Conditions. Game Length: 4 Turns (2 weeks). Play Area: All hexes on/north of hex row 40xx.

**18.2.4 Red Dragon Rising:** This scenario is the Standard Game campaign scenario. Players use the full map (including the Strategic Display), all Standard Game units (e.g., no HQs, or Air Units) in their normal setup areas, and the full Victory Conditions [12.0]. Game Length: 16 Turns (4 weeks).

**18.3 Advanced Scenarios:**

**18.3.1 Intervention Level Determination:** Before starting an advanced scenario, the Allied player chooses the Intervention Level for the US, CW, and JPN. Intervention Level VP awards are given to the non-Allied player based on the chosen levels.

**18.3.2 Intervention Levels:** The various levels of intervention correspond to the amount of force each nation is willing to risk. Level 0 – No Intervention: No forces are involved. Level 1 – Supplies, Intel, Special Operations Forces. Level 2 – Naval, Air, and Theater Weapons. Level 3 – Marines, Airborne, & US Basing Rights. Level 4 – Full Intervention: All a nation's forces.

**18.3.3 Wider Conflict:** Various actions by the non-Allied player can widen the conflict. The 2nd Korean War [18.3.3.1], Sino-Japanese War [18.3.3.2], South China Sea War [18.3.3.3], First Island Chain [18.3.3.4], and diplomacy rules are provided.

**18.3.4 Strategic Surprise – Walkin' on the Sun:** This scenario assumes that the PRC simply sets sail with what is at hand and attempts to take the island in an amphibious/airborne coup de main with follow-on forces coming out of their barracks to reinforce. Game Length: 16 Turns (4 weeks). Initial Setup: The Allied player sets up first.

**18.3.5 Tactical Surprise – Island in the Sun:** This scenario explores the middle ground between complete strategic surprise and a long buildup. The Allies are aware that the PRC is maneuvering its forces, although that really could be just an unscheduled war game. Game Length: 16 Turns (4 weeks). Initial Setup: The Allied player sets up first.

**18.3.6 Extended Buildup – Black Hole Sun:** This scenario explores the situation after tensions have been rising steadily between the PRC and the ROC. The US and Japan are highly likely to intervene with naval and air forces, and the US is more likely to commit ground units to the defense of Taiwan. This scenario is quite difficult, even impossible, for the PRC if the US has intervened. Game Length: 16 Turns (4 weeks). Initial Setup: The Allied player sets up first.

title: Advanced Game Air Unit Order of Battle
summary: Lists the air unit orders of battle for all factions in the Advanced Game: PRC, ROC, USAF, USN, USMC, JPN, CW, Malaysia, Philippines, Vietnam, and India.

title: Designer's Notes
summary: Designer Mitchell Land's notes on the unique challenges of modeling a Taiwan invasion scenario, including sea blockade, beach defenses, PRC naval SSMs, and optional rules.
text:
This edition of Next War: Taiwan adds some additional complexity to what already is the most involved game in the series. Taiwan, since it is an island, poses many challenges, both in the real world and in the cardboard one when it comes to invading and defending it.

These challenges include a potential PRC attempt to blockade the island and prevent both reinforcements and supply. The Contested Sea Movement rules and control of the sky help with the former, and I've added an entire section of rules requiring both sides to ship supplies to the island if they want to operate effectively.

The addition of ROC Beach Defenses is inspired by the Patrol Boat Tracks in Next War: Iran. They are a necessarily abstract method of portraying the numerous layers of defenses the ROC has emplaced. Unfortunately, they can't be strong everywhere at once.

Modeling the threat of the PRC Naval SSMs (DF 21/26) required some changes to bring those to the forefront, and, also, give the Allies some viable targets to reduce that threat.

Finally, there are a host of optional rules, some of which will be familiar and others which are uniquely suited to this situation that allow players to explore some nuances of current or near future warfare.

I have tried to capture and portray those challenges within the existing subsystems so that players don't have to learn whole new sub-routines. Hope you enjoy the result!
